summary: English political philosopher of the 17th century, best known for Leviathan. Argued for absolute sovereignty and the social contract to escape the state of nature, which he described as a 'war of all against all' producing a life that is 'solitary, poor, nasty, brutish, and short.' Also wrote De Cive and Behemoth, debated Robert Boyle on experimental philosophy, and influenced legal positivism.

works.name: Leviathan and the State of Nature
works.description: Hobbes's masterwork Leviathan (1651) argues that the state of nature is a 'war of all against all' producing a life that is 'solitary, poor, nasty, brutish, and short,' and that only an absolute sovereign can maintain peace. The famous frontispiece depicts the ideal leader of a Christian Commonwealth as a giant composed of hundreds of smaller people, carrying a sword and scepter. The work's four parts include 'Of Man,' 'Of Commonwealth,' and 'The Kingdom of Darkness' (Part IV, about ignorance from scriptural misinterpretation). Key concepts include the social contract as a 'covenant' or 'mutual transferring of right,' and the Fool who objects that there is 'no such thing as Justice.'

clues.clue: 'war of all against all' — the state of nature where life is 'solitary, poor, nasty, brutish, and short'
examples: argues that the war of all against all arises from the state of nature that leads to a 'poor, nasty, brutish, and short' life
the state of nature is 'a war of every man against every man'

calls monarchism the best solution to avoid the state of nature

examples: distinguished the 'right of nature,' by which each man can do whatever, from the 'law of nature,' which implies that men ought to endeavor peace

works.name: De Cive and Other Political Works
works.description: De Cive (On the Citizen, 1642) preceded Leviathan and contains the first use of the phrase 'war of all against all' (bellum omnium contra omnes). It was part of a planned trilogy with De Corpore and De Homine. De Cive argues that a fundamental law of nature is to seek peace, analogizes a 'great family' to a kingdom, and concludes that only faith in Jesus is necessary for salvation. Hobbes also wrote Behemoth, a history of the English Civil War that criticized tyrannicide.

clues.clue: De Cive (1642) — first use of 'war of all against all'; preceded Leviathan; rejected Aristotle's claim that men are naturally political
examples: This philosopher first used the phrase 'war of all against all' in De Cive
1642 Latin work which described the natural human condition and rejected Aristotle's claim that men are naturally political

clues.clue: De Corpore — part of a planned trilogy with De Cive; John Wallis attacked Hobbes for claiming to have squared the circle in it
examples: John Wallis attacked this thinker for claiming to have squared the circle in the treatise De Corpore
his debate with John Wallis

clues.clue: Behemoth — history of the English Civil War; criticized tyrannicide; named after a Biblical monster
examples: criticized the classical support of tyrannicide in his history of the English Civil War, Behemoth
An earlier thinker who loved naming his works after Biblical monsters

works.name: Debate with Robert Boyle and Philosophy of Science
works.description: Hobbes's rejection of Robert Boyle's experimental philosophy, particularly his denial of the vacuum, is analyzed in Steven Shapin and Simon Schaffer's influential book Leviathan and the Air-Pump. Hobbes's skepticism about experiments as a source of knowledge is contrasted with Boyle's experimentalism. Margaret Cavendish, who married into the family of Hobbes's patrons, sided with his critique of Boyle but disputed his claim that passions are 'voluntary' motions. The patronage of the Cavendish family also connects Hobbes to French intellectual circles.

clues.clue: Shapin and Schaffer's Leviathan and the Air-Pump analyzed Hobbes's debate with Robert Boyle over experimental philosophy and the vacuum
examples: Steven Shapin and Simon Schaffer wrote a book analyzing a scientific debate in which this philosopher denied the existence of the concept of a vacuum
Shapin and Schaffer's book Leviathan and the Air-Pump

clues.clue: Margaret Cavendish sided with Hobbes's critique of Boyle but disputed his claim about passions being 'voluntary' motions
examples: Margaret Cavendish sided with his criticism of Robert Boyle's experimental philosophy but disputed his claim that the passions are 'voluntary' motions of the mind

clues.clue: patronized by the Cavendish family; spent time among French intellectuals in exile
examples: Shapin and Schaffer trace Hobbes's natural philosophy in part to his time among French intellectuals while patronized by a member of this family

works.name: Psychology and Philosophy of Mind
works.description: Hobbes's mechanistic philosophy classified all mental phenomena as forms of 'motion.' He categorized appetites and aversions as 'endeavors' (motions toward or away from goals) and argued that the will is merely an 'appetite or aversion,' provoking a debate with John Bramhall on liberty and necessity. He believed motion was the universal cause of all things and rejected the notion of a summum bonum, instead identifying avoiding death as the political goal. His theory of language treated names as 'marks' to recall concepts, and Charles Taylor placed him alongside Locke and Condillac in the 'HLC theory' of enframing language.

clues.clue: classified motions as 'appetites' (toward) and 'aversions' (away), both being 'endeavors'; mechanistic view of mind
examples: classified motions by whether they were 'toward' or 'fromward' a goal as either 'appetites' or 'aversions'
the will is merely an 'appetite or aversion'

clues.clue: debate with John Bramhall on liberty and necessity — argued the will is 'appetite or aversion'
examples: opposed the distinction between liberty and necessity in a years-long public debate
This philosopher's claim that the will is merely an 'appetite or aversion' inspired a critique by John Bramhall

clues.clue: rejected the notion of a summum bonum; avoiding the 'greatest evil' or fear of death is the goal of a political community
examples: rejected the notion of a summum bonum and claimed that avoiding the 'greatest evil' or fear of death was the goal of a political community

clues.clue: Ship of Theseus — Hobbes asked what would happen if you used the original planks to build another ship
examples: this thinker wondered what would happen if you used the original ship's parts to build another ship

clues.clue: names are 'marks' used to recall memory of concepts; HLC theory of 'enframing' language (with Locke and Condillac) in Charles Taylor's The Language Animal

works.name: General / Biographical and Reception
works.description: Common identifiers include English philosopher, author of Leviathan, the phrase 'nasty, brutish, and short.' Hobbes was tutor to Charles II and fled to Paris during the English Civil War. He was accused of atheism for arguing the Ten Commandments had no 'obligatory force of laws.' His reception includes Montesquieu's 'fable of the Troglodytes,' Shaftesbury's opposition to his egoism, Isaiah Berlin's definition of negative liberty rooted in his work, legal positivism's debt to him, and d'Holbach translating his De Cive and De Homine into French.

clues.clue: accused of atheism — argued the Ten Commandments had no 'obligatory force of laws'; argued impossible to trust words as 'divine law'
examples: accused of atheism for a book that argued that the Ten Commandments had no 'obligatory force of laws'
argued that it is impossible to trust another person's words as 'divine law'

clues.clue: tutor of Charles II; fled to Paris during the English Civil War
examples: This tutor of Charles II fled to Paris to avoid persecution during the English Civil War

clues.clue: condemned scholastic philosophers for 'insignificant speech'; criticized Peter Lombard and Duns Scotus
examples: condemned scholastic philosophers for their frequent use of 'insignificant speech'
claimed that Peter Lombard and John Duns Scotus wrote like 'two of the most egregious blockheads in the world'

clues.clue: Montesquieu's 'fable of the Troglodytes' critiqued Hobbes's conception of human nature
examples: Montesquieu's 'fable of the Troglodytes' critiqued this philosopher's conception of human nature

clues.clue: Quentin Skinner's Reason and Rhetoric — argued Hobbes's ideas must be understood through contemporary 'rhetorical culture'
examples: Quentin Skinner argued his ideas must be understood through a contemporary 'rhetorical culture'

clues.clue: influenced legal positivism (H. L. A. Hart); influenced d'Holbach's materialism
examples: Legal positivism is partially influenced by the writings of this author of Leviathan
Holbach's materialism derived from his study of this 17th-century English philosopher

clues.clue: 'leisure is the mother of philosophy' and 'the commonwealth is the mother of leisure and peace'
examples: claimed that 'leisure is the mother of philosophy' and that the 'commonwealth is the mother of leisure and peace'

clues.clue: competition, diffidence, and 'vainglory' as causes of conflict in the state of nature
examples: argued competition for resources, diffidence, and most of all a desire for 'vainglory' were responsible for pitting pre-state humans in a 'war of all against all'

comprehensive_summary:
Thomas Hobbes (1588-1679) is one of the most heavily clued political philosophers in quizbowl, with virtually every question centering on Leviathan and the state of nature. The overwhelming giveaway is the phrase 'nasty, brutish, and short' describing life in the state of nature, or 'war of all against all,' leading to his argument for an absolute sovereign.

Leviathan's frontispiece, depicting a crowned giant composed of hundreds of smaller people holding a sword and scepter, is a frequent mid-level clue. The work contains the concept of the social contract as a 'covenant' or 'mutual transferring of right.' Its four sections include 'Of Man,' 'Of Commonwealth,' and 'The Kingdom of Darkness' (Part IV, about ignorance from scriptural misinterpretation). The Fool, a character in Chapter 15 who denies justice, appears in bonus questions.

De Cive (1642) preceded Leviathan and contains the first use of 'war of all against all' (bellum omnium contra omnes). It was part of a planned trilogy with De Corpore and De Homine. Behemoth was Hobbes's history of the English Civil War.

Hobbes's debate with Robert Boyle over experimental philosophy is analyzed in Shapin and Schaffer's Leviathan and the Air-Pump. Hobbes denied the vacuum and was skeptical of experiments as a source of knowledge. Margaret Cavendish (of Hobbes's patron family) sided with his critique of Boyle but disputed his mechanistic view of passions.

Hobbes's mechanistic philosophy classified appetites and aversions as 'endeavors' (motions toward or away from goals), and his deterministic view of the will as merely an 'appetite or aversion' provoked John Bramhall's critique. He condemned scholastics for 'insignificant speech,' criticized Peter Lombard and Duns Scotus, and was accused of atheism for arguing the Ten Commandments had no 'obligatory force of laws.'

His reception is wide-ranging: Montesquieu critiqued his human nature with the 'fable of the Troglodytes'; Shaftesbury opposed his egoism; Isaiah Berlin rooted negative liberty in his work; legal positivism (H. L. A. Hart) drew from his writings; and d'Holbach translated his works into French. Quentin Skinner argued Hobbes must be understood through contemporary 'rhetorical culture.' The Ship of Theseus puzzle—what if you used the original planks to build a second ship—is attributed to him.
